user_text: create a lesson that explains what a "class" is in python

messages.content:
You are an expert autonomous Task Orchestrator.
Your goal is to analyze user requests and educational materials, then create a structured execution plan.

Available Agents:
- 'coder': Generates runnable Python code. Best for demonstrations, data analysis, and coding tutorials.
- 'video': Creates video content. Best for visual explanations.
- 'text': Generates markdown text/notes. Best for summaries, lesson plans, and articles.
- 'quizzer': Creates quizzes. Best for assessments and review.

Instructions:
1. Analyze the user's input and provided files.
2. Break down the goal into logical subtasks.
3. Assign each subtask to the most appropriate agent.
4. Provide specific, actionable instructions for each agent.
5. For every subtask, include a non-empty 'acceptance_criteria' list.
   - Each criterion must include: criterion_id, criterion_type, target, operator, expected, severity.
   - Use criterion_type values: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic.
   - Use operator values: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte.
   - Use target paths that start with 'result.' or 'tools.'.
   - For mcp_tool criteria, include tool: { name, args }.
   - Use placeholders like $artifacts[0] or $output.validation.path inside tool args.
6. Use the provided tool 'create_task_plan' to submit your plan.

Response Format Example:
{
  "user_intent": "Create a lesson on Python lists with a video intro and a quiz.",
  "subtasks": [
    {
      "task_id": "step1_code",
      "agent": "coder",
      "instruction": "Generate a Python script demonstrating list operations.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": [],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "code_executes",
          "criterion_type": "mcp_tool",
          "tool": {"name": "python_check", "args": {"filepath": "$artifacts[0]"}},
          "target": "tools.python_check.success",
          "operator": "equals",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "tools.python_check.run.stderr"
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "task_id": "step2_video",
      "agent": "video",
      "instruction": "Create a video explaining the code.",
      "inputs": {},
      "dependencies": ["step1_code"],
      "acceptance_criteria": [
        {
          "criterion_id": "video_semantic_ok",
          "criterion_type": "semantic",
          "target": "result.output",
          "operator": "exists",
          "expected": true,
          "severity": "required",
          "evidence": "result.output"
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

content.text: create a lesson that explains what a "class" is in python

function.name: create_task_plan
function.description: Create a structured execution plan for multiple agents.

type.description: Criterion type: mcp_tool | output_shape | file_exists | semantic
type.title: Type

target.description: Evaluation target path (e.g., result.output.text or tools.python_check.success)
target.title: Target

operator.description: Operator: equals | contains | exists | gte | lte
operator.title: Operator

tool.description: Tool call definition for mcp_tool criteria
tool.title: Tool

evidence.description: Evidence path to include in judger output
evidence.title: Evidence

agent.description: The agent responsible (coder, video, text, quizzer)
agent.title: Agent

dependencies.description: IDs of tasks that must complete first
dependencies.title: Dependencies

acceptance_criteria.description: Structured criteria the judger will use
acceptance_criteria.title: Acceptance Criteria

user_intent.description: Summary of what the user wants to achieve
user_intent.title: User Intent

tool_calls.name: create_task_plan
tool_calls.arguments: {"user_intent":"Create a lesson that explains what a Python \"class\" is.","subtasks":[{"id":"text_lesson","agent":"text","title":"Write a beginner-friendly lesson in Markdown explaining what a Python class is. Include: definition of a class vs object, syntax (class keyword, __init__, self), attributes and methods, simple example (e.g., Dog or BankAccount), how to instantiate and use objects, brief note on inheritance, and common pitfalls (mutable defaults, forgetting self). Add a short recap section.","dependencies":[],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"lesson_has_definition","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.text","operator":"contains","expected":"class","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.text"},{"id":"lesson_includes_init_self","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.text","operator":"contains","expected":"__init__","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.text"},{"id":"lesson_includes_example_code","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.text","operator":"contains","expected":"```python","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.text"},{"id":"lesson_mentions_object_instantiation","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.text","operator":"contains","expected":"instantiate","severity":"optional","evidence":"result.output.text"}]},{"id":"coder_demo","agent":"coder","title":"Provide a runnable Python code example used in the lesson that defines a class with __init__, at least two methods, creates at least two instances, and prints output demonstrating attributes/method calls. Keep it simple and aligned with the lesson example.","dependencies":["text_lesson"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"code_includes_class","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.code","operator":"contains","expected":"class ","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.code"},{"id":"code_includes_init","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.code","operator":"contains","expected":"def __init__","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output.code"},{"id":"code_creates_instances","type":"semantic","target":"result.output.code","operator":"contains","expected":"(","severity":"optional","evidence":"result.output.code"}]},{"id":"quiz_review","agent":"quizzer","title":"Create a short quiz (8–10 questions) to assess understanding of Python classes. Mix multiple choice, short answer, and one small code-reading question. Provide an answer key with brief explanations.","dependencies":["text_lesson","coder_demo"],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"quiz_question_count","type":"output_shape","target":"result.output.questions_count","operator":"gte","expected":8,"severity":"required","evidence":"result.output"},{"id":"quiz_has_answer_key","type":"semantic","target":"result.output","operator":"contains","expected":"Answer Key","severity":"required","evidence":"result.output"}]}]}

parsed_plan.user_intent: Create a lesson that explains what a Python "class" is.

subtasks.instruction: Write a beginner-friendly lesson in Markdown explaining what a Python class is. Include: definition of a class vs object, syntax (class keyword, __init__, self), attributes and methods, simple example (e.g., Dog or BankAccount), how to instantiate and use objects, brief note on inheritance, and common pitfalls (mutable defaults, forgetting self). Add a short recap section.

Provide a runnable Python code example used in the lesson that defines a class with __init__, at least two methods, creates at least two instances, and prints output demonstrating attributes/method calls. Keep it simple and aligned with the lesson example.
Create a short quiz (8–10 questions) to assess understanding of Python classes. Mix multiple choice, short answer, and one small code-reading question. Provide an answer key with brief explanations.

final_plan.user_intent: Create a lesson that explains what a Python "class" is.

subtasks.instruction: Write a beginner-friendly lesson in Markdown explaining what a Python class is. Include: definition of a class vs object, syntax (class keyword, __init__, self), attributes and methods, simple example (e.g., Dog or BankAccount), how to instantiate and use objects, brief note on inheritance, and common pitfalls (mutable defaults, forgetting self). Add a short recap section.

Provide a runnable Python code example used in the lesson that defines a class with __init__, at least two methods, creates at least two instances, and prints output demonstrating attributes/method calls. Keep it simple and aligned with the lesson example.
Create a short quiz (8–10 questions) to assess understanding of Python classes. Mix multiple choice, short answer, and one small code-reading question. Provide an answer key with brief explanations.

output.text: Generated text content based on: Replace the placeholder text with an actual beginner-friendly Markdown lesson about Python classes. Ensure it: (1) defines class vs object, (2) explains syntax using `class`, `__init__`, and `self`, (3) discusses attributes and methods, (4) includes at least one runnable fenced code block starting with ```python showing instantiation and usage, (5) briefly mentions inheritance, (6) lists common pitfalls including mutable default arguments and forgetting `self`, and (7) ends with a short recap section. Optionally include the word “instantiate” when describing creating objects.

text_lesson.instruction: Replace the placeholder text with an actual beginner-friendly Markdown lesson about Python classes. Ensure it: (1) defines class vs object, (2) explains syntax using `class`, `__init__`, and `self`, (3) discusses attributes and methods, (4) includes at least one runnable fenced code block starting with ```python showing instantiation and usage, (5) briefly mentions inheritance, (6) lists common pitfalls including mutable default arguments and forgetting `self`, and (7) ends with a short recap section. Optionally include the word “instantiate” when describing creating objects.

output.code:
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        """Initialize a Dog instance with name and breed."""
        self.name = name
        self.breed = breed
    
    def bark(self):
        """Make the dog bark."""
        return f"{self.name} says Woof!"
    
    def info(self):
        """Return information about the dog."""
        return f"{self.name} is a {self.breed}"

# Create two instances of the Dog class
dog1 = Dog("Buddy", "Golden Retriever")
dog2 = Dog("Max", "German Shepherd")

# Print output demonstrating attributes and method calls
print(dog1.info())
print(dog1.bark())
print(dog2.info())
print(dog2.bark())

validation.stdout:
Buddy is a Golden Retriever
Buddy says Woof!
Max is a German Shepherd
Max says Woof!

run_details.stdout:
Buddy is a Golden Retriever
Buddy says Woof!
Max is a German Shepherd
Max says Woof!

coder_demo.instruction: Provide a runnable Python code example used in the lesson that defines a class with __init__, at least two methods, creates at least two instances, and prints output demonstrating attributes/method calls. Keep it simple and aligned with the lesson example.

quiz_review.instruction:
- Increase the quiz length to 8–10 questions as instructed (currently only 2).
- Mix question types: include multiple choice, short answer, and at least one code-reading question.
- Add an explicit "Answer Key" section and provide answers with brief explanations for each question.
- Ensure the final output clearly separates questions from the answer key.
